title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: The finding description explicitly states that enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported and that if the rule fails on an installed system, it is a permanent finding. Modifying dracut configurations to enable FIPS involves kernel-level changes and boot-time modules which can lead to boot failure or system instability if not handled during initial installation.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Kernel-level configuration change; Potential for boot failure; FIPS mode enablement is not supported on preexisting systems; Changes affect the initramfs/boot process

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode is a fundamental change to the system's cryptographic subsystem and kernel behavior. The finding description explicitly states that enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported and requires kernel-level changes. Such changes can break existing applications that rely on non-compliant cryptographic algorithms and can lead to system instability or boot failure.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Kernel parameter changes; Cryptographic policy enforcement; Potential breakage of non-FIPS compliant applications; System instability during boot

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: The description explicitly states that enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported and requires a kernel boot flag (fips=1) during installation. Attempting to force this on a running system can lead to unpredictable cryptographic behavior or system instability, and the rule itself notes that if it fails on an installed system, it is a permanent finding that cannot be fixed via standard remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode changes core cryptographic provider behavior; Requires kernel boot parameter changes not supported post-installation; Potential for system instability or application failure due to restricted algorithms

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: This finding is a compliance check regarding the lifecycle status of the OS. It cannot be 'remediated' via a technical configuration change or script; it requires a human to verify the support contract or upgrade the OS to a supported version if it is End-of-Life.
triage_estimated_impact: Cannot be automated via configuration management; Remediation may involve a full OS upgrade/reinstall

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: The remediation involves deleting user accounts. If an automated process or an administrator deletes a service account or a user currently being used for remote access (SSH) or application execution, it will cause immediate service disruption or loss of access. A human must verify the list of users before deletion.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Accidental deletion of service accounts; Accidental deletion of administrative/SSH accounts; Disruption of application dependencies relying on specific local users

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: This finding involves modifying the GRUB2 bootloader configuration and generating a new grub.cfg. Any error in bootloader configuration or failure to correctly set the superuser/password can result in a system that is unbootable or inaccessible via the console, requiring physical/out-of-band recovery. Per policy, changes to the bootloader are classified as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential for system boot failure; Risk of losing console access to the bootloader; Requires manual verification of grub.cfg generation

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: While setting a GRUB2 password is a standard hardening step, it involves the bootloader configuration. If the password is lost or the configuration is corrupted during the process, the system may become unbootable or require physical/console access to recover, potentially leading to downtime.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for bootloader misconfiguration; Risk of lockout from boot-time menu editing if password is lost

title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
triage_reason: While adding DNS servers is generally safe, modifying /etc/resolv.conf can lead to immediate loss of network connectivity or name resolution if incorrect IP addresses are provided. In modern RHEL 9 systems, /etc/resolv.conf is often managed by NetworkManager or systemd-resolved; manual edits may be overwritten or cause conflicts with the network management stack.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential loss of network connectivity if incorrect DNS IPs are used; Conflict with network management services (NetworkManager/systemd-resolved); Potential breakage of service discovery/name resolution

title: Disable the use of user namespaces
triage_reason: While the sysctl change itself is a simple configuration edit, disabling user namespaces can break container runtimes (like Podman or Docker) and certain sandboxing features in applications. The description explicitly warns that containers require a non-zero value, necessitating a manual check of the workload environment before application.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential breakage of containerized workloads (Podman, Docker); Disruption of application sandboxing mechanisms

title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
triage_reason: While the change is a standard Postfix configuration hardening, modifying 'smtpd_client_restrictions' can break legitimate mail relaying workflows for internal applications or services that rely on the mail server to forward mail to external domains. A human must verify that 'permit_mynetworks' covers all necessary trusted sources.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential disruption of legitimate mail relaying for internal applications; Changes to mail delivery workflow

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The finding involves standardizing the configuration of the Chrony NTP client by ensuring it uses the 'server' directive. This is a common hardening practice for time synchronization and does not impact system access, networking connectivity, or filesystem integrity.
triage_estimated_impact: Incorrect NTP server configuration could lead to time drift if the new server directive points to an invalid source
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i 's/^pool /server /' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd; chronyc tracking; chronyc sources
review_feedback: The plan correctly addresses the specific requirement to replace 'pool' directives with 'server' directives to comply with the hardening rule. Converting 'pool' to 'server' is functionally valid for Chrony, although 'pool' is often preferred for larger datasets; however, for this specific compliance requirement, the plan is correct.
review_concerns: Replacing a 'pool' directive with a 'server' directive might result in the client connecting to only one address from the pool instead of multiple, which can slightly impact synchronization reliability if the pool was large, but it strictly satisfies the stated requirement.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan directly addresses the compliance requirement by converting 'pool' directives to 'server' directives. While 'pool' is technically more efficient for large sets of NTP servers, 'server' is a valid and functional directive in chrony. The risk to system stability or access is negligible.
qa_side_effects: Potential reduction in NTP source diversity if a large pool is reduced to a single server entry via improper sed substitution, though chrony will still function.

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: Enabling Certmap in SSSD requires specific, domain-dependent configuration entries in sssd.conf. Applying a generic or incorrect certmap rule can break authentication for users or groups relying on SSSD, potentially leading to a lockout from the system.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrect configuration in sssd.conf can break identity/authentication services; Requires knowledge of specific domain/certificate mapping requirements; Potential for user lockout if the matchrule or maprule is misconfigured
